panels.markdown:
# Rewrite prfait from scratch

prfait is meant to be a tool that helps reduce the burden brought on by higher output from AI assisted coding.

Initially, this focused on the *reading* side. Unfortunately, the impact scoring was just inaccurate enough to be useless, and it was just buggy and slow enough to deter usage.

This PR deletes that all, and instead focuses on the *authorship* of a PR.

If it becomes easy to write high quality PRs, guided by a human with understanding of the whole narrative, the hope is that this reduces the burden on the reviewer.

# Workflow

You begin by running `prfait init`, which places github action in your repo.
Then, each time you create a branch and want to open a PR, you can run `prfait new` (or `prfait new [branch]` to use a different comparison target).

This starts a small local tool, and opens an editing experience in your browser.

Think of this like literate programming, or a workbook like Jupiter. You can write an explanation of your PR, and easily grab snippets of code, like this one:

which handles adding specific selected text snippets into a storyboard.

This story then provides a narrative for the PR. The author can focus the reviewers attention on the right areas, making it easier to understand **The Point.**

In the future, these snippets might include images, diagrams, or even be interactive.

comments.markdown: I can even leave inline comments on the code, letting me seamlessly transition from prose-first to code-first communication styles.

panels.markdown:
# Opening a PR

These files get saved as `.prfait/[branch-name].json`; when you open a pull request, the github action runs and updates your PR description with a markdown rendered version of your story.

In the future, this can also link to a hosted version of the editing UI, for better code reviews.
And use that to render the PR description with the appropriate language. We only use `diff` fencing if any lines were deleted.
And finally, the terminal was breaking escape sequences, so arrow keys and backspace stopped working, so this seems to have fixed that:
